chunks.text: The Association for Computing Machinery (ACM) has named Sylvia Ratnasamy, Professor of Computer Science at UC Berkeley, to its 2025 class of ACM Fellows. Ratnasamy is being recognized “for contributions to networks and networked systems.”
chunks.question: Why was Sylvia Ratnasamy named an ACM Fellow in 2025?
chunks.answer: For contributions to networks and networked systems.

chunks.text: Ratnasamy is widely known for her seminal work on Distributed Hash Tables (DHTs). As a doctoral student, she co-authored the landmark paper “A Scalable, Content-Addressable Network,” which introduced a decentralized method for locating data in large-scale networks without a central registry. This innovation has had a profound impact on the design of modern peer-to-peer systems and cloud infrastructure.
chunks.question: What is Sylvia Ratnasamy's key contribution to computing?
chunks.answer: Seminal work on Distributed Hash Tables.

chunks.text: Ratnasamy joins a cohort of 71 new Fellows honored for their “remarkable results through technical innovation and service to the field.” This year’s honorees represent institutions across 14 countries, working in fields ranging from AI for healthcare to sustainable computing.
chunks.question: How many ACM Fellows were named in 2025?

chunks.text: “These men and women represent the top 1% of professionals in our association,” said ACM President Yannis Ioannidis. “We hope that their work will serve as an inspiration to the next generation.” The 2025 Fellows will be formally recognized at an awards banquet on June 13 in San Francisco.
chunks.question: When will the 2025 ACM Fellows be formally recognized?
chunks.answer: At an awards banquet on June 13.
